label: ai
topic: A father with Parkinson's collapses the night of a rocket launch
text:
The launch is scheduled for 9:47 p.m., and your father wants to watch it from the porch. He has been talking about it all week, this particular rocket, a cargo resupply mission to the space station, which is not the most glamorous kind of launch but is, he insists, the most beautiful, because the rocket is lighter without a crew and climbs faster, a bright needle threading the dark.

You help him into the chair on the porch. This is a production now. The Parkinson's has progressed to the point where sitting down is a negotiation between his body and gravity, a series of micro-adjustments, his hand on your forearm, his weight shifting in increments. He lowers himself with the careful deliberation of someone defusing a bomb. Once seated, he exhales as if he has accomplished something significant. He has.

This is three weeks after his third spinal surgery. The surgeon was optimistic, which you have learned to translate as: the surgery went well; the disease did not get the memo. Your father's spine is a cathedral of titanium hardware now, rods and screws and fusion points, and still his hands shake when he lifts a glass of water, still his feet shuffle on the linoleum as if the floor cannot be trusted.

The porch faces east. You can see the causeway, the strip of land and light between here and the launch pad, twenty miles across the water. The air is warm and smells like salt and jasmine from the bush your mother planted the year your parents moved here, which was the year your father was diagnosed, which was the year everything became a before and after.

"Tell me about the rocket," you say. You do not want to talk about his back. You do not want to talk about the appointment on Thursday or the new medication or the physical therapist who comes three times a week and leaves looking discouraged.

"It's a Falcon 9," he says. "Reusable first stage. The booster comes back and lands on a drone ship in the Atlantic." He is animated now, his voice stronger than it has been all day. This is what space does for him. It lifts him out of the chair, out of the body that is failing him, into a place where physics is elegant and things work the way they are supposed to.

He tells you about orbital mechanics, about the Tsiolkovsky rocket equation, about the specific impulse of liquid oxygen and kerosene. You understand perhaps a third of it. It does not matter. What matters is the way he leans forward, the way his hands move as he talks, the tremor still there but secondary, a background vibration beneath the signal of his enthusiasm.

"Two minutes," he says, checking his phone.

You watch the horizon. The sky is clear, a deep navy, and the stars are visible in a way they never are in the city. You think about your father in the hospital three weeks ago, his back opened for the third time, the surgeon navigating between vertebrae that have been fused and refused, and you think about how the body is a kind of spacecraft too, a pressurised vessel carrying a person through an environment that is trying to kill them, and how maintenance is constant, and how eventually the systems fail regardless.

A glow appears on the horizon. Then the rocket itself, a column of white fire rising from the tree line, climbing with a speed that makes your breath catch. The sound arrives seconds later, a low rumble that you feel in your sternum. Your father is gripping the arm of the chair, leaning forward, his face lit by the distant fire.

"There it goes," he says quietly.

The rocket arcs eastward, the flame shrinking, the trail of exhaust catching the last light. It is impossibly beautiful. It is a controlled explosion carrying supplies to six people floating two hundred and fifty miles above you. It is a thing humans built because they refused to accept the limitations of the ground.

Your father stands. He does this without asking for help, which is how you know something is wrong. He pushes himself up from the chair with both hands and takes one step and then his legs fold beneath him, not dramatically, not like falling, but like a building being demolished in slow motion, each floor collapsing onto the one below. He goes to his knees, then to his side, and you are there, your hands on his shoulders, saying his name.

"I'm all right," he says. He is not all right. His face is grey. His breath is shallow. You can feel the tremor in his shoulders through your palms.

You call your mother. You call the ambulance. You sit on the porch with his head in your lap and the rocket's exhaust trail still fading in the sky above you, a white line drawn on the dark, already dissolving.

At the hospital, they run tests. They adjust medications. They use words like episode and precautionary and monitor. Your father lies in a bed that is too short for him, wires taped to his chest, and asks you if the booster landed successfully.

You look it up on your phone. It did. It landed on a drone ship called Of Course I Still Love You, which is a name borrowed from a science fiction novel, which is the kind of detail your father would appreciate.

"It landed," you tell him.

He closes his eyes. He smiles. Outside the window, the sky is dark and full of objects in motion — satellites, stations, debris, all of it falling and missing the ground, which is all that orbit is. You hold your father's hand and watch him sleep and think about return journeys, about the booster falling back through the atmosphere, finding the ship, coming to rest. About how the hardest part of any mission is not the launch. It is coming home.
